name: Lyft Driver Location
overview:
The Driver Location Service continuously tracks the real-time GPS positions of all active Lyft drivers. It must
ingest location updates from hundreds of thousands of drivers every few seconds, store the latest position for
fast lookup, maintain a geospatial index for proximity queries (find drivers near a given point), and stream
location updates to riders tracking their driver. The system is write-heavy and latency-sensitive.

inScope: Ingest driver location updates every 4 seconds
Query nearest available drivers within a radius
Stream driver location to rider during active ride
Store 24 hours of location history per driver

items: Support 500k active drivers with 125k location writes/sec
99.99% availability — location tracking outages directly break ride matching
Eventual consistency for location data; stale positions up to 4 seconds are acceptable
24-hour location history per driver retained for analytics and dispute resolution

entities.name: DriverLocation
entities.schema:
{
  driver_id: string,
  lat: float,
  lng: float,
  heading: float,
  speed: float,
  timestamp: ISO8601,
  status: enum(AVAILABLE|ON_TRIP|OFFLINE)

}

endpoints.response: { drivers: [{ driver_id, lat, lng, distance_km, eta_minutes }] }

approaches.title: PostgreSQL with PostGIS
approaches.description: Store driver locations in PostgreSQL with PostGIS geometry columns. Use ST_DWithin for radius queries. 500k drivers × 1 update/4s = 125k writes/sec. PostgreSQL cannot sustain this write load. PostGIS radius queries on 500k rows take >100ms.
approaches.tradeoff: Write throughput too high; query latency too slow.

approaches.title: Redis GEOADD/GEORADIUS
approaches.description: Use Redis GEOADD to store driver locations. GEORADIUS queries find nearby drivers in O(N+log M). Redis handles 125k writes/sec easily. GEORADIUS on 500k drivers completes in <5ms. Single Redis instance is a bottleneck; no persistence for location history.
approaches.tradeoff: Single Redis instance bottleneck; no location history.

approaches.title: Redis Geo (sharded by geohash) + Cassandra history
approaches.description: Shard Redis by geohash prefix (4 chars = ~40km cells). Each shard handles a geographic region. Location history written asynchronously to Cassandra (partition key = driver_id, clustering key = timestamp DESC). Rider tracking uses a dedicated Redis pub/sub channel per active ride.
approaches.tradeoff: Cross-shard queries for drivers near cell boundaries; Cassandra adds write path complexity.

components.name: Location Ingest API
components.responsibility: Receives driver heartbeats; validates; writes to Redis Geo + publishes to

components.name: Redis Geo (sharded)
components.responsibility: Geospatial index for driver positions; sharded by geohash prefix

components.name: Kafka
components.responsibility: Streams location updates for rider tracking and history archival

components.name: Proximity Service
components.responsibility: Queries Redis Geo for nearby drivers; returns sorted by distance

components.name: Rider Tracking Service
components.responsibility: Subscribes to Kafka for active ride driver updates; pushes to rider via

components.name: WebSocket Gateway
components.responsibility: Maintains rider connections; receives driver location pushes

questions.title: 2. Rider Tracking During Active Ride
questions.content:
When a ride starts, the Rider Tracking Service subscribes to a Kafka topic partition for the driver's driver_id.
Every location update from the driver is published to Kafka and consumed by the Rider Tracking Service, which
pushes it to the rider's WebSocket connection. The rider sees the driver's position update every 4 seconds.
When the ride ends, the subscription is cancelled.

questions.title: 3. Driver Status Management
questions.content:
Driver status (AVAILABLE/ON_TRIP/OFFLINE) is stored in Redis alongside the location. When a driver goes
offline (no heartbeat for 30 seconds), a background job marks them as OFFLINE and removes them from the
geospatial index. This prevents stale driver positions from appearing in proximity queries.

questions.title: 4. Location Smoothing
questions.content:
Raw GPS coordinates have noise (±10 meters). The Location Ingest API applies a Kalman filter to smooth
driver positions before storing them. This reduces jitter in the rider's tracking view and improves ETA accuracy.

The Kalman filter uses the driver's previous position, speed, and heading to predict the next position and
correct it with the GPS measurement.

questions.title: 5. Capacity Planning
questions.content:
500k drivers × 1 update/4s = 125k writes/sec. Redis GEOADD is O(log N). With 20 Redis shards, each shard
handles ~6,250 writes/sec — well within Redis's 100k ops/sec capacity. Kafka handles 125k messages/sec
with 10 partitions at 12.5k messages/sec each. Cassandra handles 125k writes/sec with a 10-node cluster at
12.5k writes/sec per node.

keyFlows: ① Driver sends GPS every 4s → Location API → Redis Geo (GEOADD)
② Location API publishes to Kafka → Cassandra (history) + Tracking Svc
③ Rider requests nearby drivers → Proximity Svc → Redis GEORADIUS
④ Active ride: Tracking Svc streams driver location → WebSocket → Rider
⑤ Driver offline (30s no heartbeat) → removed from Redis Geo index

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study geohash in depth: the base32 encoding, how precision levels map to cell sizes, and the
neighbor calculation algorithm for finding adjacent cells.
Expert Note: Learn about Kalman filters for GPS smoothing: the predict-update cycle, how to tune the
process noise and measurement noise covariance matrices.
Expert Note: Understand Redis Geo internals: how GEOADD stores coordinates as 52-bit integers in a
sorted set, and how GEORADIUS uses the sorted set for range queries.
Expert Note: Study WebSocket connection management for real-time tracking: how to handle connection
drops and reconnects without missing location updates.
Expert Note: Learn about Cassandra's time-series data model: how to design partition keys to avoid hot
partitions, and how to use TTL for automatic data expiration.
